_about: Leading / biased-language detector. Lowers a claim's reliability when it substitutes persuasion technique for evidence. Grounded in real marketing/psychology/sociology research (citations per category). PRINCIPLE: detect the TECHNIQUE, not the political position — the us_vs_them list is deliberately symmetric (left- and right-coded pejoratives both included). DOUBLE-EDGED categories (emotion, intensifiers) are weighted low and combined with the evidence dimensions, never used as a sole veto — high-arousal language can be legitimate (a real emergency is emotional).

_scoring.load_formula: load = sum over categories of min(cap, hits * weight)
_scoring.density: load_per_100 = load * 100 / max(words, 30)   (short text not extrapolated past a 30-word base)
_scoring.discount: final_weight = evidence_weight * (1 - 0.6 * manipulation_score)   (heavy manipulation costs up to 60%)
_scoring._note: Params are tunable and SHOULD be calibrated against the Da San Martino Propaganda Techniques Corpus (PTC) in Phase 2. v0 values are reasoned defaults.

_key_findings.Brady_2017_PNAS: Each additional moral-emotional word increased message diffusion ~20% (n=563,312); contagion bounded within ideological groups (echo chambers).
_key_findings.TverskyKahneman_1981: Logically-equivalent gain/loss framing reverses preferences by exploiting System-1 / loss aversion.
_key_findings.Entman_1993: Framing = selection + salience to promote a particular interpretation.
_key_findings.DaSanMartino_2019: 18 propaganda techniques annotated at span level in news (Propaganda Techniques Corpus); loaded language and name-calling are the most frequent.
_key_findings.GanterStrube_2009: Weasel words flagged via Wikipedia weasel tags + shallow features — vague attribution that implies authority without evidence.
_key_findings.Cialdini_1984: Six persuasion levers (authority, social proof/bandwagon, scarcity, reciprocity, liking, commitment) used in marketing.
_key_findings.BlomHansen_2015: Clickbait uses forward-reference, second-person 'you', superlatives, numerals, attention punctuation.

categories.name: Loaded / emotionally charged language
categories.definition: Words chosen for emotional charge rather than description, to influence the reader (Da San Martino's most frequent technique).
categories.citation: Da San Martino et al. 2019 (EMNLP-IJCNLP); MPQA Subjectivity Lexicon (Wilson et al. 2005)

categories.name: Appeal to fear / threat
categories.definition: Builds support by stoking fear of an alternative or an existential threat.
categories.citation: Da San Martino et al. 2019 ('Appeal to fear/prejudice'); Witte EPPM 1992

categories.name: Us-vs-them / name-calling / labeling
categories.definition: Labels a person/group with a name the audience fears or loves, to bypass argument. SYMMETRIC list by design.
categories.citation: Da San Martino et al. 2019 ('Name calling/Labeling','Flag-waving'); Brady et al. 2017 (group-bounded contagion)

categories.name: Weasel words / vague attribution
categories.definition: Vague, unnamed authority that implies support without evidence ('experts say', 'some believe').
categories.citation: Ganter & Strube 2009 ('Finding Hedges by Chasing Weasels')

categories.name: Exaggeration / minimization / superlatives
categories.definition: Representing something as far greater or smaller than it is; superlative-driven clickbait.
categories.citation: Da San Martino et al. 2019 ('Exaggeration/Minimisation'); Blom & Hansen 2015

categories.name: Absolutes / overgeneralization
categories.definition: All-or-nothing claims and false certainty that erase nuance (black-and-white framing).
categories.citation: Da San Martino et al. 2019 ('Causal oversimplification','Black-and-white fallacy')

categories.name: Moral-emotional / outrage language
categories.definition: Combined moral + emotional words that drive viral diffusion within in-groups (+20%/word).
categories.citation: Brady et al. 2017 (PNAS, 'Emotion shapes the diffusion of moralized content')

categories.name: Clickbait / sensational hooks
categories.definition: Forward-reference and curiosity-gap hooks engineered for clicks over information.
categories.citation: Blom & Hansen 2015; Biyani et al. 2016; SENS-HEAD (sensational-headline dataset)
markers: what they don't want you to know

categories.name: Thought-terminating cliché
categories.definition: Stock phrases that shut down analysis rather than invite it.
categories.citation: Lifton 1961; Da San Martino et al. 2019 ('Thought-terminating cliché')

categories.name: Bandwagon / social proof
categories.definition: Urges acceptance because 'everyone' is doing/believing it.
categories.citation: Cialdini 1984 (Influence, social proof); Da San Martino et al. 2019 ('Bandwagon')

categories.name: Intensifiers / boosters
categories.definition: Degree words that amplify without adding evidence (mild signal).

categories.name: Loss/gain framing (partial)
categories.definition: Equivalency framing that exploits loss aversion. Hard to lexicalize fully; light markers only — full detection deferred to the NLP phase.
